GIS in Airfield Maintenance

The Evolution of GIS in Airfield Maintenance

←→
Scroll
Early 1990s
Airports Rely on CAD Drawings and Paper Maps

Airports manage pavement with disconnected CAD drawings and paper records

Pre-GIS Era
Seminal Research
1991
GIS Pavement System at JFK, LGA, EWR

Schwartz et al. develop IAPMS, the first GIS-based airfield pavement management system

Engineers query any airfield section and instantly access its maintenance data

1995
McNerney — 58% of Airports Plan GIS Adoption

McNerney's survey reveals airports are ready but lack a standardized GIS framework

His report becomes the roadmap pushing GIS toward industry-wide adoption

Seminal Research
Federal Standard
2007 — 2009
FAA Launches Airports GIS Program

David Bennett leads development of federal GIS data standards

The FAA establishes GIS as the federal standard for airport data collection and submission

2010
GIS Moves from Desktop to Field

Esri Smart Airfield Solutions brings GIS inspections to handheld devices

Paper FAA Part 139 processes replaced by live digital data capture

Field Innovation
Industry Milestone
2013
Esri Releases ArcGIS for Aviation: Airports

First GIS platform built specifically for airport operations

Brings FAA compliance, pavement tracking, and obstacle analysis into one solution

2019
GIS 3D Digital Twins Validated as Airport Standard

Schiphol Airport case marks industry-wide recognition of GIS-based 3D digital twins

Airports model and manage operations in a live 3D spatial environment

Emerging Technology
Most Recent
2025 — Present
GeoAI Enters Airport GIS

AI takes digital twins from passive monitoring to active prediction

Airports forecast disruptions and model future scenarios before they occur

×
